items.prompt: According to Example 12.23, what do global streaming platforms combine for scale?
choices: Object storage, CDNs, and real-time pipelines
Only a single desktop hard drive
Only offline optical discs with no network
items.explain: Example 12.23 cites object storage, CDNs, and real-time pipelines under concurrent load.

items.prompt: Example 12.24 says music platforms can update recommendations without relying only on nightly batch warehouses.
items.explain: Example 12.24 streams listening events into real-time analytics so charts and recommendations update live.

items.prompt: Where does Example 12.25 place large DICOM imaging archives?
choices: Only anonymous public FTP without controls
Only handwritten film labels with no digital store
items.explain: Example 12.25 stores DICOM archives in object storage with governed access.

items.prompt: Name one analytics framework or store mix mentioned for music-platform scale in Example 12.24.
items.rubric: Accept SQL and NoSQL mix, Apache Spark, cloud storage, or real-time analytics.
items.sample_answer: A mix of SQL and NoSQL with Apache Spark (or cloud storage plus real-time analytics).

items.prompt: The case studies invent entirely new stacks unrelated to earlier storage and streaming patterns in the chapter.
items.explain: Section 12.5 presents the cases as compositions of formats, consistency, versioning, and lineage already covered.
